value: Financial planning professionals under FPAM must adhere to the CFP Code of Ethics and Professional Responsibility. FPAM establishes standards for ethical conduct and professional practice in Malaysia.

Individuals offering financial planning services in Malaysia must hold a Capital Markets Services Representative's License (CMSRL) under the Capital Markets and Services Act 2007, regulated by the Securities Commission.
Malaysia's household debt-to-GDP ratio stood at 84.2% as of late 2024. Debt-service-ratio (DSR) thresholds are carefully monitored by BNM to maintain financial stability.
The median monthly household income in Malaysia was RM6,338 based on the latest HIES. Spending on housing, utilities, and transport constitutes over 45% of average household expenditure.
AKPK recommends that a healthy Debt-Service-Ratio (total monthly debt installments divided by net monthly income) should not exceed 30% to 40% of net take-home income.

EPF reports indicate that a significant share of members exhaust their retirement savings within 5 years of full withdrawal at age 55, highlighting the critical need for structured annuities or drawdown plans.
The basic savings target set by EPF for members at age 55 is RM240,000. This is calculated to provide a minimum retirement income of RM1,000 per month for 20 years.
Average life expectancy in Malaysia is 72.5 years for males and 77.4 years for females as of 2024 data, implying a retirement duration of 15 to 25 years.
If a non-Muslim dies intestate (without a will) in Malaysia, their assets are frozen and distributed according to the strict formulas of the Distribution Act 1958, rather than their personal wishes.
Under the Distribution Act 1958 (amended 1997) for non-Muslims, if a person dies leaving a spouse, children, and parents: Spouse receives 1/4, Children receive 1/2, Parents receive 1/4 of the estate.

For a will to be valid under the Wills Act 1959, the testator must be at least 18 (Peninsular Malaysia), write it in writing, sign it in the presence of 2 concurrent witnesses who are not beneficiaries.
For Muslims, estate distribution is governed by Syariah Faraid inheritance laws. Up to 1/3 of the estate can be bequeathed via Wasiyah (will) to non-heirs, while 2/3 must follow Faraid allocations.
source: Department of Syariah Judiciary Malaysia Guidelines

value: Malaysia does not currently levy estate duty or inheritance tax on deceased estates. However, Real Property Gains Tax (RPGT) may apply to subsequent transfers of property.
Under SC guidelines, wholesale fund investments are restricted to sophisticated investors, defined as individuals whose net personal assets exceed RM3 million or annual income exceeds RM300,000.
source: SC Guidelines on Unlisted Capital Market Products

value: Malaysia operates a single-tier dividend system (dividends are tax-exempt to shareholders) and does not tax individual capital gains on equities, making equities highly tax-efficient for HNW individuals.
The statutory minimum employer EPF contribution rate in Malaysia is 13% for employees earning RM5,000 or below, and 12% for employees earning above RM5,000.
Employers must contribute to SOCSO (Social Security Organization) and EIS (Employment Insurance System) for statutory employee accident and loss of employment coverage based on wage tables.
Employers with 10 or more Malaysian employees are mandated to register with HRD Corp and pay a monthly human resources development levy of 1% of their employees' monthly wages.

BNM Guidelines on Product Transparency and Disclosure require financial advisors to provide a Product Disclosure Sheet (PDS) highlighting fees, charges, key benefits, and risk terms clearly before product purchase.
source: BNM Insurance and Takaful Market Conduct Guidelines
